Remote work took off during COVID and never went back. Three years in, most of the debate has moved past "does it work?" and into "how do we make it work better?"

The short answer on productivity: it depends. A 2024 Stanford study found hybrid workers were about 5% more productive than full-time office staff, measured by output metrics like code commits and support ticket closures. But those numbers flatten out for roles that depend on spontaneous collaboration. If your job is mostly heads-down focused work, remote is probably better. If you're onboarding junior engineers, maybe not.

Retention is where the numbers get more interesting. Companies offering flexible arrangements see meaningfully lower turnover. That tracks with what I hear anecdotally too. People don't want to commute an hour each way for work they can do from their kitchen table. The ones who do want an office tend to want it two or three days a week, not five.

The cost savings are real but uneven. Some companies have cut office space by 40% and pocketed the difference. Others spent it all on stipends, co-working memberships, and flying people in for quarterly off-sites. Whether remote work actually saves money depends on how you restructure around it.

The biggest unsolved problem is still informal mentorship. Hallway conversations, overhearing a senior engineer debug something, absorbing culture by proximity. None of that translates well to Zoom. Teams that figure this out will have an edge. Most haven't yet.

Remote work is staying. The details are still getting sorted out.
